role_summary: First African-American federal judge in South Carolina (1979); civil rights litigator behind the 1963 Harvey Gantt integration of Clemson University and the 1972 South Carolina reapportionment case; first African-American attorney from the Deep South appointed to the federal judiciary (1976, U.S. Court of Military Appeals).

photo.alt: Matthew J. Perry Jr., painted portrait, U.S. District Court for South Carolina
photo.photographer: United States District Court for the District of South Carolina (portrait painted by Larry Francis Lebby)

ai_reading: Perry's top semantic neighbor is Robert L. Carter (entry 108) at a cosine count of 378, the second-strongest paired-lawyer signature in the catalog (Carter is also Becton's #1 neighbor at 753 and Clarence B. Jones's #1 at 246). Second-strongest is Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. (entry 39) at 194, the future South Carolina Chief Justice whose page is built around the parallel South Carolina civil-rights-legal network. The embedding therefore reads Perry as a node in the SC legal cohort that also includes Cecil J. Williams (entry 14, count 57, the photographer who documented their cases) and Chuck McDew (entry 16, count 35, the SNCC chairman whose South Carolina State protests Perry's litigation overlapped with). The novel observation is the position on the Individual Conscience vs Collective Discipline axis, where Perry sits at +0.70 (normalized): for a federal judge whose institutional posture is all collective discipline (precedent, rules, courtroom procedure), the embedding pulls his testimony the other way, toward Individual Conscience, the pole of personal moral witness and the autobiography of one's own decisions. That cuts against the role rather than confirming it, and in a corpus where the other audited judicial and lawyer voices land lower on the same positive side of the axis (Greenberg +0.12, Finney +0.13, Carter +0.32, Clarence B. Jones +0.58, all normalized), Perry's +0.70 makes him the most conscience-weighted of the legal voices in the audited subset of the catalog. The reading tracks the texture of his testimony, which recounts the individual confrontations of his own career (the Spartanburg judge he handed a pre-drafted notice of appeal, the bar exam his class alone failed) rather than the committee machinery of movement organizations. He reads deep Southern at -0.68 (normalized) on the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis, consistent with a litigation career fought entirely in South Carolina courts.
biographical_paragraph: Perry was born August 3, 1921 in Columbia, South Carolina, and died July 29, 2011 [src: 3, 4]. He took both his undergraduate B.S. (1948) and his law degree (1951) at South Carolina State College in Orangeburg, in the first graduating class of the new state-run Black law school [src: 3, 4]. As chief counsel for the South Carolina NAACP from 1957, his cases included the 1963 Gantt v. Clemson litigation, argued with Constance Baker Motley, that produced the first African-American enrollment at Clemson College; the Rackley v. Tri-County Hospital litigation arising from Gloria Blackwell Rackley's October 1961 arrest for sitting in a whites-only hospital waiting room in Orangeburg, after which the hospital was integrated; the 1963 Edwards v. South Carolina argument before the U.S. Supreme Court protecting civil rights marchers; and the 1972 South Carolina reapportionment case that pressed legislative redistricting toward the "one man, one vote" principle [src: 2, 3]. President Gerald Ford nominated him to the U.S. Court of Military Appeals in 1976; President Jimmy Carter nominated him to the U.S. District Court for the District of South Carolina, where the Senate confirmed him on September 19, 1979, the first African American to hold a federal district judgeship in the state [src: 3, 4]. He assumed senior status in 1995 and served until his death [src: 4]. Congress had already designated the federal courthouse to be built in Columbia as the Matthew J. Perry Jr. United States Courthouse by Public Law 103-360 in 1994; the building opened in 2004 [src: 8, 9]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 87 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: On a wartime weekend pass, waiting more than two hours for a connecting train in Birmingham, Perry could not enter the terminal restaurant where Italian prisoners of war were being served:
interview_snippets.quote: But as I looked and as I contemplated here I was at that time an American soldier. I was wearing the uniform of my country. I had just gone through basic training. I had been trained to go into a matter involving our engagement with the enemy.

interview_snippets.speaker: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recalling how Thurgood Marshall regarded the separate-but-equal law school South Carolina opened at South Carolina State rather than admit Black students to the University of South Carolina, the school Perry then attended:
interview_snippets.quote: In fact, Thurgood Marshall referred to it as a Jim Crow dump.

interview_snippets.speaker: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: South Carolina ended its diploma-privilege exemption just as Perry's class graduated, requiring the bar examination for the first time; of his small class, he was the only one to pass:
interview_snippets.quote: And we had to take the examination. By the way, I was the only person who passed of the group. Now, whether the examination was fairly administered or anything like that, I cannot tell you, but I can tell you I studied my can off for the thing.

interview_snippets.speaker: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: A Spartanburg municipal judge had been convicting Perry's clients on sight; on a later appearance Perry came prepared with a pre-drafted notice of appeal and asked the judge to accept service of it:
interview_snippets.quote: So he was shocked, he looked up at me and he says, what is this? How did you know what my decision was going to be? I said, Your Honor, I said, this has been your decision every time I've come here.

interview_snippets.speaker: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the Columbia bus case of Sarah Mae Fleming Brown, which Perry took to trial and which he dates ahead of the Montgomery litigation that followed Rosa Parks:
interview_snippets.quote: But Sarah Mae Fleming Brown's case was the first one on the record.

interview_snippets.speaker: Cecil J. Williams
interview_snippets.lead_in: Photographer Cecil J. Williams rode in the small motorcade that carried Harvey Gantt to register at Clemson, with Perry as lead counsel:
interview_snippets.quote: I had been a part of a convoy I think of about three cars and one involving Matthew J. Perry Jr again Matthew J. Perry Jr and Constance Baker Motley had assisted Gantt in gaining entrance to the Clemson University.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr., the future South Carolina Chief Justice, places Perry at the center of the state's civil rights legal network as its chief counsel:
interview_snippets.quote: But as I recall, it I. DeQuincey Newman was actively involved in whatever Chief Counsel Perry was involved in,

movement_context: Perry took his law degree at the South Carolina State College School of Law in Orangeburg in 1951, the state-funded Black law school the South Carolina General Assembly chartered in 1947 as its separate-but-equal answer to the NAACP-coordinated litigation pressing for African-American admission to the all-white University of South Carolina law school [src: 5]. The same Orangeburg law-school cohort produced Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. (catalog entry 39, JD 1954), who later became the first African-American Chief Justice of the South Carolina Supreme Court since Reconstruction, and a generation of state-network civil rights lawyers who would carry the bulk of the post-Brown litigation across South Carolina through the 1960s and 1970s [src: 5]. Perry became a cooperating attorney for the NAACP Legal Defense Fund in the 1950s and was named chief counsel to the South Carolina NAACP in 1957, the lead civil rights attorney for the state through the 1960s [src: 2, 3]. He worked downstream from the Clarendon County school-desegregation case Briggs v. Elliott, which Thurgood Marshall and the LDF first tried before a three-judge district court in 1951 and which was later consolidated with cases from three other states into Brown v. Board of Education, decided in 1954 [src: 6]. Perry, newly graduated from law school, was in the courtroom for the Briggs trial in Charleston; the Briggs lineage made South Carolina one of the jurisdictions whose challenge to Plessy reached the Supreme Court in Brown, and the post-Brown work of forcing implementation across South Carolina school districts, hospitals, public accommodations, and voting maps fell substantially to Perry's litigation docket [src: 6]. In the spring of 1961, as the lunch-counter sit-in movement spread across the upper South, Perry's litigation cohort and Finney's defense work in Rock Hill ran parallel: Finney represented the Friendship 9 students at the McCrory's variety-store sit-in case in York County, while Perry handled the LDF-coordinated litigation on the integration cases that followed across the state [src: 7]. President Gerald Ford nominated Perry to the U.S. Court of Military Appeals in 1976, making him the first African-American attorney from the Deep South appointed to a federal judgeship [src: 2, 4]. In 1979 President Jimmy Carter nominated him to the U.S. District Court for the District of South Carolina, where the Senate's confirmation made him the first African-American U.S. district judge appointed in the Deep South [src: 3, 4].
legacy_and_reception: Congress designated the federal courthouse to be built at 901 Richland Street in downtown Columbia, South Carolina as the Matthew J. Perry Jr. United States Courthouse through Public Law 103-360, enacted October 14, 1994, while Perry was a sitting judge; the building opened in 2004 to house the U.S. District Court for the District of South Carolina on which Perry sat from 1979 to 1995 and then as Senior Judge until his death [src: 8, 9]. The Official South Carolina Hall of Fame inducted him in 2007 [src: 10]. He died on July 29, 2011 in Columbia, South Carolina at the age of eighty-nine, his service on the federal bench having continued in Senior Judge status from his 1995 transition until his death [src: 1, 4]. The University of South Carolina Press published the scholarly biography Matthew J. Perry: The Man, His Times, and His Legacy in 2004 (edited by W. Lewis Burke and Belinda F. Gergel), and Perry's papers are held at the South Caroliniana Library of the University of South Carolina [src: 2].

gallery.alt: Matthew J. Perry Jr. United States Courthouse, Columbia, South Carolina
gallery.caption: The Matthew J. Perry Jr. United States Courthouse at 901 Richland Street in Columbia, South Carolina. Congress designated the courthouse for Perry through Public Law 103-360, enacted October 14, 1994, while he was a sitting judge; the building opened in 2004. It houses the U.S. District Court for the District of South Carolina on which Perry sat from 1979 to 1995, then as Senior Judge until his 2011 death. The naming made Perry one of the few federal judges to have a courthouse named in his honor during his lifetime. Photograph released by the United States District Court, District of South Carolina.
gallery.photographer: United States District Court, District of South Carolina

gallery.alt: Lowman Hall, South Carolina State University, Orangeburg, viewed from the north
gallery.caption: Lowman Hall on the campus of South Carolina State University in Orangeburg, photographed April 26, 2015 from the north. The SC State College School of Law operated from this campus from its 1947 chartering by the South Carolina General Assembly as the state's separate-but-equal answer to the NAACP-coordinated litigation pressing for African-American admission to the all-white University of South Carolina law school. Perry took his undergraduate B.S. (1948) and his JD (1951) at South Carolina State, in the law school's first graduating class; Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr. (catalog entry 39) took his JD here in 1954. The SC State law school closed in 1966 once the desegregation of South Carolina's white law schools made the separate facility redundant.

Elliott in Clarendon County, the South Carolina case first tried before a three-judge district court in 1951 and consolidated into Brown v. Board of Education in 1954, the doctrinal substrate from which Perry's SC civil rights litigation descended)

sources.title: Kim Cliett Long, The Friendship 9: A Civil Rights Story of Sit-Ins, Activism, and the Power of "Jail, No Bail"
